title: Roy Rises from the Ashes to Win Miami Thriller as Audi's Shadow Hangs Over the Podium
meta: Published: 10 May 2026 | By Elena Vasquez, Chief Race Correspondent | F1CC News
caption: Jason Roy took a stunning victory in Miami — his first of the season — as Red Bull finally delivered on their promise.
body:
The sun was setting over the Hard Rock Stadium, the Miami sky streaked with orange and purple, when Jason Roy crossed the finish line and broke a drought that had lasted far too long. The Red Bull driver, who started 12th, who had watched his car catch fire in Jeddah, who had asked "why are we still here" as the flames licked at his rear wing, won the Miami Grand Prix. He led the final laps under pressure from Zhao Lei's McLaren and Eren Aygen's Williams, and when the chequered flag fell, he let out a scream that carried all the frustration of a season that had promised so much and delivered so little — until now.

But the story of this race was not just Roy's redemption. It was the shadow that hung over the podium, the investigation into Audi that could yet strip both their cars of their results. It was the crash that ended Jack TEF's stand‑in career in the wall. It was the scream of Maikel Zwart, still echoing from Jeddah, still searching for answers. And it was the radio messages — raw, unfiltered, human — that told the truth of a race that shifted with every lap.

Eren Aygen started from pole, his Williams flying through the Miami heat after a qualifying session that saw him beat Michiil MaraDöner by just five milliseconds. But the race is not qualifying, and Aygen's advantage evaporated slowly, then all at once. He led the early laps, holding off MaraDöner and the two Audis, but the medium tyres on Richard Hicks' car began to sing in the Florida heat. By lap 21, Hicks had taken the lead. By lap 24, Aygen was fighting just to stay on the podium.

The first safety car arrived on lap 32. Wesley Vandesteene, who had been running in the points, lost the rear of his Mercedes and slammed into the wall at Turn 8. The field dived for the pits. Strategies shattered. When the race resumed, Roy — who had already climbed from 12th to the fringes of the top five — found himself in second place, his Red Bull on fresh tyres, his eyes on Hicks' Audi ahead. He took the lead on lap 47 with a move of breathtaking commitment around the outside of Turn 1, and he never gave it back.

Then came the red flag. Jack TEF, the Williams team principal who had driven so brilliantly in his stand‑in role, lost control and hit the wall. The McLaren of — the broadcast didn't specify which McLaren — was involved. TEF's radio message was a sigh of exhaustion: "God damn guys... DNF. Sorry. Koop you mind taking one more race out so I can finish on a high?" The reply from Lucas Koopman, still recovering from his back injury, was instant: "Uhhhh no." TEF's stand‑in career ended not with a podium, but with a wrecked car and a joke between friends. He will hand the car back to Koopman in Canada, his legacy secure: a podium in Bahrain, a fourth in Jeddah, and the respect of a paddock that watched a team principal drive like a professional.

The restart was a five‑lap sprint. Roy controlled it with the authority of a driver who had decided he would not be denied. Zhao Lei, who had started eighth and driven a race of relentless precision, climbed to second. Aygen held off a ferocious charge from the Audis to secure third. And then, as the cars crossed the line, the waiting began. Audi — both cars — are under investigation. The results are provisional. Hicks finished fourth, Keisner fifth, but the paddock whispers suggest the podium could yet change.

Behind the top five, the race was a tapestry of small victories and quiet disappointments. Eetu Väisänen finished fourth — later promoted to fourth if the Audi situation changes — and extended his championship lead in a weekend where he never looked like winning. Quarter Kevu drove his damaged Racing Bulls to eighth, a result that felt like a victory. Julian Kondra finished seventh, his second consecutive points finish for Alpine. Philippe Verhaeren took the final point in tenth. And Sarah Springfield — P18 on the road, the last of the running cars before the DSQs — posted a tweet that captured the weariness of a driver fighting a car that won't fight back: "P18 and last of all cars running." She later gained two positions from the Audi disqualifications, but the zero points remained.

Maikel Zwart finished tenth — a single point, his first since the China sprint. It was not the redemption he had screamed for in Jeddah, but it was something. A step. A reason to keep believing. His radio silence after the race was louder than any scream could have been.

The championship has tightened once more. Väisänen still leads, but Keisner — provisional or not — is closing. Zhao Lei's second place vaults him further into contention. Aygen remains in the fight. And Roy, the man who asked why he was still here, now has a victory to his name. Red Bull are still a long way from where they expected to be, but for one Miami evening, they were exactly where they needed to be: on the top step, bathed in the sunset, the weight of a difficult season lifted, at least for a moment.

The paddock heads to Canada. Koopman returns. TEF goes back to the pit wall. Audi wait for the stewards' verdict. And the championship, as always, waits for no one.
